data.title: Justin Sun's "plastic friendship" with the Trump family has backfired.
data.description:
Written by: Yangz, Techub News

Justin Sun, who once called Trump a "benefactor of the crypto industry," has completely turned against him in the past two days.

On the afternoon of April 12, Justin Sun suddenly published an article attacking World Liberty Financial (WLFI), a DeFi project backed by the Trump family. He made a series of accusations, claiming that the WLFI team never obtained authorization for community governance but privately extracted fees from users, secretly implanted backdoors to control assets, froze investor funds without disclosure and due process, and even treated the entire crypto community as an "ATM."

... A few hours later, WLFI responded decisively, asking, "Does anyone still believe in Justin Sun?" and adding, "See you in court." Justin Sun immediately retaliated, demanding that the project's responsible party, as the project's largest investor, come forward under their real names instead of hiding in the shadows. This public spat came suddenly, but a closer look reveals that the feud between the two sides didn't begin overnight. To understand why Justin Sun is now flipping the table, we must first look at how fervently he supported WLFI in the past. Once, they were "family." Let's rewind to the end of 2024. At that time, WLFI's tokens weren't selling well; almost no one was buying them. Just when the project team was in a bind, Justin Sun appeared, dressed in a suit and tie. In November 2024, he invested $30 million through an affiliated company, instantly becoming WLFI's largest investor at the time, accounting for more than half of the total funds raised. WLFI officials were very supportive, directly hiring him as a project advisor, praising his "insights and experience as crucial." This wasn't all. In January 2025, on the eve of Trump's inauguration, Justin Sun invested an additional $45 million, stating bluntly, "If I make money in the crypto industry, all the credit should go to President Trump." Following this, Justin Sun's relationship with the Trump family rapidly warmed. In May 2025, Justin Sun again made a significant investment, purchasing Trump's Meme token, becoming its "number one holder." This transaction earned him an extremely rare ticket: a private dinner at the Trump National Golf Club in Virginia. At that dinner, Justin Sun was not only presented with a $100,000 custom gold watch from the Trump brand as a symbol of his status as a "top fan," but was also invited to give a speech on stage. He thanked Trump for his support of the crypto industry on camera, and the atmosphere was very harmonious for a time. More intriguingly, about two weeks after this dinner, the SEC's civil fraud and securities violation lawsuit against Justin Sun and his three companies was temporarily suspended by the presiding judge in the Southern District of New York. Subsequently, Tron announced plans to list in the US through a reverse merger with Nasdaq-listed SRM Entertainment, a deal orchestrated by Dominari Securities, an investment bank with ties to the Trump family. In July, Justin Sun again pledged to purchase $100 million worth of Trump tokens. This inevitably led to speculation: the official menu at the dinner was lackluster, but the hidden menu was quite extensive. Honeymoon Over: Locked Wallets

However, the good times didn't last. In September 2025, the sweet storyline suddenly took a sharp turn for the worse.

At that time, on-chain data monitoring showed that Sun Yuchen's associated address transferred approximately 50 million WLFI tokens (worth about $9 million at the time) to his HTX exchange. The market instantly panicked, and the price of WLFI tokens plummeted from $0.19 to $0.16.

Sun Yuchen quickly tried to quell the controversy, saying that he was only "testing the exchange's deposit process" and had no intention of selling.

Sun Yuchen quickly came out to try to cover it up, saying that he was only "testing the exchange's deposit process" and had no intention of selling.

However, the WLFI team clearly doesn't see it that way. After the transfer occurred, the team blacklisted Sun Yuchen's three core wallet addresses under the pretext of "protecting investors," claiming that a total of 272 addresses were frozen, and that this wasn't targeted at Sun Yuchen personally. According to monitoring, Sun Yuchen's frozen assets included 595 million unlocked WLFI tokens (worth approximately $107 million at the time), plus 2.4 billion tokens still locked. Justin Sun panicked and publicly called for the "unlocking of my tokens," even promising to purchase $10 million worth of ALTS and $10 million worth of WLFI (Alt5 Sigma is WLFI's treasury company) through the market, attempting to prove to the Trump family that he was a staunch long-term holder. However, WLFI ignored him. Sun later deleted his promised tweet. Why did it take half a year to finally break the ice? After being frozen, Justin Sun didn't immediately escalate the conflict. In the following months, he remained restrained, perhaps still harboring illusions. After all, real money was involved, and a falling out wouldn't benefit him. Moreover, seeing Zhao Changpeng receive a "full pardon" from Trump, he might also be hoping that his heavily invested "friendship" could be exchanged for a pardon in return. On March 5th of this year, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) formally applied to the Manhattan Federal Court to dismiss all civil fraud charges against Justin Sun, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation. Although Sun received a $10 million fine and the case was dropped, rather than a presidential pardon like CZ's, the biggest regulatory cloud hanging over his head finally dissipated. However, after this alarm was lifted, Sun discovered that his "ally," WLFI, was becoming increasingly dangerous. In early April, on-chain monitoring showed that the WLFI team had pledged 5 billion of its own tokens to borrow $75 million worth of stablecoins from Dolomite, transferring some of the funds to Coinbase Prime, raising suspicions of a "cash-out and run" scheme. Coupled with the fact that the price of WLFI had fallen to approximately $0.07 by then, his assets of over $100 million had shrunk by more than 50%. Since a dignified recovery of his assets was out of the question, maintaining the empty title of "advisor" would only make him look like a sucker. Sun Yuchen probably thought that rather than waiting to be killed, he should completely overturn the table, expose everything to the public, and force the other party to take action. Thus, the public attack on April 12th occurred. After Sun Yuchen's attack on WLFI, Banteg, an anonymous developer from Yearn Finance, pointed out that WLFI's initial contract was not so "domineering." The blacklist function was "quietly added" through a V2 version upgrade 11 months after Sun Yuchen's investment and a week before trading opened. Even more chillingly, the project team subsequently added an asset seizure function—ostensibly to "deal with phishing hackers," but in reality, it gave them the physical power of "confiscation." Even more problematic is WLFI's lock-up mechanism. Its contract code even includes a separate category specifically for Justin Sun. As the sole user in this category, Sun did not enjoy the normal linear unlocking process. The project initially offered the lure of "20% unlocking at the start," but after he used this small portion of his funds, they quickly activated a blacklist, permanently locking up the remaining 80% of his assets. Furthermore, its governance structure is absurd: freezing an address only requires a single guardian address signature, while seizing assets requires a 3/5 multi-signature vote. In other words, a single person has the unilateral power to freeze the assets of any token holder. Justin Sun subsequently retweeted this analysis and demanded that WLFI disclose the identities of the administrators hiding behind the EOA accounts and the 3/5 multi-signature system. Conclusion As of this writing, WLFI has not offered any substantive response to Justin Sun's accusations or Banteg's on-chain analysis, aside from the statement "See you in court." This "See you in court" formally marks the bankruptcy of Justin Sun's political gamble, which lasted over a year and cost nearly $100 million. He once thought he had bought a license to the core of American politics. In the end, he discovered that he had only purchased a string of cold, hard code written into a blacklist. This enormous lesson may have brought him the dissipation of regulatory clouds, but it has certainly made him see the arrogance of power. Whether the two sides will actually go to court is no longer important. This farce of "investor tearing apart project team" is enough to sound an alarm for all investors who blindly trust celebrity endorsements: in the crypto world, trust often costs hundreds of millions, while a backdoor that takes everything from you only requires a single line of free code.

data.title: Stability is an illusion: the crash of 75 years ago is being repeated in the stablecoin market.
data.description:
Author: Prathik Desai Source: tokendispatch Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Stability is often an illusion, especially in the financial world. You bet on a seemingly mundane financial product, expecting it to bring stable and reliable returns, and it does indeed perform as expected, until the underlying fundamentals falter. These kinds of "sound investments" are often more deceptive than speculative targets; people already assume the latter carries risk, but rarely anticipate that seemingly safe choices will go wrong.

About 75 years ago, history played out this scenario.

In the 1940s, after the Great Depression and World War II, European banks accumulated large amounts of dollar deposits. Depositors could hold dollars in non-US banks to hedge against the risk of their own currencies depreciating. These deposits offered considerable returns and spurred many innovative ideas: US companies and other depositors began to hold dollars overseas to circumvent domestic capital controls.

European banks readily accepted these offers, lending them out at even higher interest rates.

The flood of dollar deposits in Europe ultimately gave rise to the Eurodollar market—a parallel dollar system unregulated by the Federal Reserve. With the onset of the Cold War in the late 1940s, the situation began to spiral out of control. More and more people demanded the return of their dollars, but banks didn't have enough physical dollars on hand, and the entire system collapsed. A similar logic is playing out in the stablecoin market today, except that the issuers of digital dollars seem to have learned from history. This in-depth analysis will explore whether Ethena's reliance on a traditional stock market model can salvage its stablecoin reserve strategy.

The Road to Success

In early 2024, Ethena launched its synthetic stablecoin USDe, taking a completely different path from the industry. USDe is pegged to the US dollar, but it does not need to hold real US dollars in bank reserves. Instead, for every $1 USDe minted, it corresponds to holding crypto assets such as Bitcoin and Ethereum, while simultaneously shorting an equivalent amount of crypto futures.

These two positions hedge against each other: when Bitcoin rises, the spot profit is offset by the loss from the futures short position, and vice versa. Ultimately, even if there are no real US dollars in the account, USDe can always maintain a value of $1.

Why would users abandon mainstream stablecoins like USDT and USDC in favor of USDe? The answer is the profit incentive. This incentive mechanism is perfectly suited to the rules of the crypto derivatives market: in a bull market, most traders are bullish, and exchanges charge long positions a continuous small funding fee, which is then paid to short positions. Ethena consistently holds short positions, collecting this fee and distributing it as profit to USDe holders. Its peak annualized return once exceeded 20%. Within 18 months of its launch, USDe's circulating supply increased sevenfold to approximately $15 billion, setting a record for the fastest growth rate in stablecoin history. However, this design is highly dependent on the crypto market maintaining its current state. While shorting during a bull market can be profitable for those in the minority, market trends always reverse. On October 11, the cryptocurrency market experienced its largest liquidation in history, with over $19 billion evaporating. The following day, USDe briefly de-pegged, falling to as low as $0.65 on Binance. In the five months since October 10, the circulating supply of USDe has plummeted from approximately $15 billion to below $6 billion. A belated lesson? Over $90 billion in USDe was redeemed. Perpetual futures, which once almost 100% supported the reserve model, now account for only 11%. Ironically, all of this could have been avoided; Ethena should have been warned much earlier. Markets are cyclical, and the crypto space is no exception, as repeatedly demonstrated over the past 16 years. Over-reliance on perpetual futures, deeply tied to market movements, as a single collateral is a ticking time bomb. Other stablecoin issuers have already begun to adjust: with the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates, the two leading stablecoin institutions have expanded their reserve revenue channels. Tether has significantly increased its gold reserves for diversification, while USDC issuer Circle is heavily investing in infrastructure revenue through its Layer 1 public chain Arc and the full-stack internet payment system Circle Payments Network. Ethena, however, has been slow to act. Its founder, Guy Young, admitted on social media that the decline would have been far more severe without timely intervention. He also listed the adjustments Ethena has taken to adapt to market changes: USDe reserves are being upgraded to reduce concentration, and cross-cycle resilience is being built through diversified collateral.

A Solution Toward Traditional Finance

Ethena plans to expand its collateral base to include stock and commodity basis trading, overcollateralized lending, commodity brokerage services, and more real-world assets (RWAs).

Ethena initially positioned itself as a crypto-native synthetic dollar, unlike USDT and USDC which are backed by real dollars or short-term treasury bonds. Now, it has circled back to the traditional financial system to continuously provide returns for holders, and is developing multiple sources of income.

The stock basis trading model is consistent with its previous crypto asset strategy: buy S&P 500 spot while shorting the corresponding futures to profit from the price difference. This type of return, though small, is stable and unaffected by crypto market volatility.

Similarly, Ethena can replicate this strategy in commodities such as gold, silver, wheat, and crude oil, as well as various indices and lending markets, executing neutral arbitrage to profit from price differences around the clock, without needing to consider retail investor sentiment towards cryptocurrencies.

While this move reduces reliance on the crypto market, it shifts the focus to markets like stocks and commodities. If these markets experience increased volatility or futures liquidity dries up, the strategy could also fail, further squeezing USDe returns. However, this concern equates to the belief that a diversified cross-asset portfolio will collapse entirely. While such a scenario is possible, it is extremely rare. Financial markets are based on probability and mathematical logic; the core of diversification is not to profit against the trend, but to reduce the probability and magnitude of losses. This is precisely Ethena's purpose in diversifying its portfolio with multiple unrelated sources of return. Liquidity Test Diversification is a reasonable solution to market cycles. Diversifying across stocks, commodities, credit, and crypto assets makes the return stream more resilient, which is its core advantage compared to USDT and USDC, which rely on short-term government bonds and offer no returns. However, the new strategy still faces significant challenges. USDe's liabilities are fully liquid, allowing holders to redeem at any time; however, the liquidity of yield-generating assets is not ideal when the market is under pressure. Equity basis positions require time to be smoothly closed out, institutional loans have fixed terms, and debt-backed securities often face insufficient liquidity in volatile markets. This mismatch between liquid liabilities and imperfectly liquid assets is a structural contradiction in all interest-bearing stablecoins, which even diversification strategies cannot completely resolve. In stable markets, various assets move independently: inflation concerns drive up gold prices, positive earnings boost the stock market, geopolitical crises in oil-producing countries push up oil prices, and crypto funding rates remain high due to retail investor bullishness. However, in extreme risk events, asset correlations break down, the advantages of diversification vanish, and liquidity becomes the core link between all assets. Once the market deteriorates across the board, all investors will choose to exit and cash out. Harry Markowitz won the Nobel Prize for his theory of diversification to reduce risk, but the 2008 financial crisis proved that this theory has exceptions. Scholar Nassim Nicholas Taleb also pointed out in *The Black Swan* that asset correlations are not constant and change with market conditions. However, such black swan events are inevitable and extremely rare, almost impossible to predict or control. Diversified asset allocation remains superior to concentrated holdings in a single asset; the 2008 money market crash serves as an example: at that time, reserve funds held short-term corporate bonds instead of government bonds to pursue interest rate spreads, and after Lehman Brothers' collapse, these bonds became worthless overnight. Over-collateralization is one of Ethena's solutions: borrowers collateralize assets exceeding the loan amount, theoretically allowing them to absorb losses before they impact USDe holders. However, the over-collateralization ratio is set based on historical volatility ranges, and extreme market conditions may exceed this range. No strategy can completely eliminate risk. What Ethena needs to do is convince investors that this new diversification strategy is more robust than its previous reliance on a single crypto market.

data.title: AI multi-agent applications will boost the rise of blockchain payments.
data.description:
What advantages does blockchain have over mobile payments? This is a question that has embarrassed many blockchain experts. In 2015, renowned blockchain evangelist Andreas Antonopoulos was challenged by an audience member during a speech: mobile payments are convenient and fast, while Bitcoin is slow and cumbersome; what advantages does it have over other payment methods? He didn't defend himself, but instead presented a vivid scenario: if a driverless taxi operated independently, needing to collect fares and pay for its charging, wouldn't Bitcoin be a more ideal payment solution?

This is a thought-provoking question. In fact, over the past decade, many thinkers in the blockchain field, such as Zhu Jiaming and Xiao Feng, have considered this question and raised a bold question: perhaps blockchain was never intended for humans, but rather for AI and robots.

Ten years ago, AI agents and driverless cars were still cutting-edge research in laboratories, but now, these technologies are being applied in various ways.

Currently, the development of AI multi-agent systems has become a hot topic. Will it open up new avenues for blockchain applications and ultimately lead to the integration of AI and blockchain? Multi-agent systems are becoming the hottest AI application direction. Starting in the second half of 2025, multi-agent systems suddenly became one of the most popular directions for AI implementation. A significant proportion of the most watched AI projects recently belong to this direction. - Anthropic, a leader in the Agentic AI field, has continuously launched products such as Cowork, Agent Teams, and Managed Agents, clearly demonstrating its leadership in this direction. Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) provides a standardized framework to help developers quickly build layered, scalable multi-agent systems, supporting parallel, sequential, and cyclic orchestration. OpenClaw allows ordinary users to deploy a "Lobster team" on their local machines, collaborating to complete multi-step workflows, sparking heated discussions about "AI employees" and "one-person companies." ByteDance's DeerFlow 2.0, after being open-sourced, quickly topped GitHub Trending. It's a super-agent runtime infrastructure that orchestrates sub-agents, long-term memory, and Docker sandboxes to autonomously complete long-duration, complex tasks ranging from minutes to hours, completely solving the pain point of traditional AI's "manual handover." - Gary Tan, CEO of Y Combinator, a well-known Silicon Valley incubator, open-sourced gstack, turning Claude Code into a virtual startup team. It includes 23 professional roles such as CEO, designer, engineer, QA, and security officer, along with slash commands, allowing one person to simulate the operation of an entire startup. - The TradingAgents project, a collaboration between UCLA and MIT, allows multiple agents to act as fundamental analysts, sentiment analysts, technical analysts, and risk managers, making investment decisions through debate and collaboration, simulating the organizational processes of a real trading company. - Paperclips.AI focuses on organizing a group of agents into a complete architecture capable of autonomously operating a company, including organizational charts, budgets, governance, and goal setting, achieving a closed-loop business model with zero human intervention. These projects collectively point to a clear trend: people are no longer satisfied with individual AI assistants, but are beginning to build real work teams and collaborative networks using multi-agent architectures. In the past, we mainly used AI to "ask questions and get answers"; now we are starting to let AI "work as a team"—dividing tasks, collaborating, supervising each other, and making autonomous decisions to complete complex tasks that only human organizations can handle. This is not a simple tool upgrade, but a crucial turning point for AI, moving from a personal efficiency tool to an organizational-level technology. The core of a multi-agent system is that multiple agents are no longer isolated "role-playing," but form a dynamic network that can create accounts, scale, recombine, and even collaborate across organizational boundaries based on task requirements. They can handle high-frequency, small-scale, cross-entity, and cross-jurisdictional value exchanges, while triggering complex contract enforcement conditions. These characteristics naturally necessitate a completely new payment and transaction infrastructure for multi-agent organizations. Why must multi-agent applications be equipped with a new payment system? When AI multi-agent organizations move from the laboratory to real-world tasks, payment and value exchange are no longer optional add-ons, but rather the lifeblood of the system. Once a multi-agent network begins processing actual business, it generates a large number of high-frequency, small-scale, cross-entity, and even cross-jurisdictional payment demands. Agent A might complete content generation in seconds, and Agent B immediately needs to call a paid model for it; Agent C, after processing logistics data, needs to immediately pay Agent D for data usage; in cross-border collaborations, an agent in Singapore might need to pay an agent on a US server for computing power. These payments can occur dozens of times per minute, with amounts as small as 0.1 cents, and the participants may be completely different organizations or individuals. These exchanges are often accompanied by complex value flows: not only money, but also data, computing power, model access rights, fragments of intellectual property, etc. More importantly, the dynamism of multi-agent organizations far exceeds that of traditional organizations. Intelligent agent accounts can be created at any time, and organizations can scale and recombine at any time. One minute a task might require a small team of five intelligent agents, while the next minute another task could require twenty agents to be instantly reorganized, some even from external partners. When intelligent agents across organizations make payments to each other, complex contract execution conditions inevitably arise, with payments only automatically executed when thresholds are reached. These conditions can be nested, multi-layered, and real-time, defying traditional contracts and beyond the real-time response capabilities of traditional banking systems. Traditional banking systems are ill-suited for this scenario. They are accustomed to large-scale, batch, manually reviewed netting settlements, with response times measured in hours or even days. They cannot provide instant account opening services for thousands of constantly created and destroyed intelligent agent accounts, cannot handle the complex conditions attached to each transaction, and cannot offer the kind of "personalized service"—intelligent agents need code-level, millisecond-level automated execution, not phone calls to customer service or paper applications. Bank rules are designed for people, and processes are designed for stable institutions. However, when faced with a globally distributed network of intelligent agents that can instantly reorganize, never sleep, and operate at full speed, its limitations become immediately apparent. The advantages of blockchain as a next-generation financial market infrastructure are highlighted in this scenario. Blockchain is essentially a distributed ledger, allowing all participants to share the same real-time updated public ledger without repeated reconciliation. Smart contracts directly translate contract terms into code, automatically executing once conditions are met, without third-party intervention. Programmability transforms payments from simple transfers into automated processes that can incorporate arbitrarily complex logic: triggered upon condition fulfillment, executed atomically, and rolled back upon failure. Full settlement on a transaction-by-transaction basis replaces traditional netting, with each transaction clearing and settling simultaneously upon confirmation. Atomic cash-on-delivery ensures that value transfer and asset delivery occur simultaneously, preventing default by either party. Instantaneous finality means that once a transaction is recorded on the blockchain, it is irreversible and tamper-proof. These characteristics are a near-perfect fit with the operational logic of multi-agent organizations. Agents need to create accounts at any time, and the cost of generating blockchain addresses is close to zero; agent organizations need to scale constantly, and smart contracts can deploy new rules instantly; cross-organizational collaboration requires complex triggering conditions, and smart contracts are inherently designed for this; high-frequency micro-payments require low-cost, instant settlement, and blockchain's gas fees and Layer 2 solutions are reducing costs to negligible levels. Traditional infrastructure is centralized, rigid, and slow, while blockchain is disintermediation-free, flexible, and real-time. We are increasingly seeing that multi-agent organizations are not simply piecing together AI tools, but rather building a completely new collaborative paradigm. This paradigm places unprecedented demands on payment and transaction infrastructure, and blockchain is currently the only mature technology system that can meet these requirements. It is not icing on the cake, but an infrastructure-level necessity. When AI agents truly begin to work as a team, blockchain will no longer be an option, but a necessity. Multi-agent applications will become the "base" for blockchain payments. In traditional C2C payment scenarios, blockchain's performance is not outstanding. For ordinary people, transferring money via WeChat or Alipay takes only seconds—entering the amount and scanning a code for confirmation. Blockchain wallets, however, require copying addresses, verifying gas fees, and waiting for block confirmation, resulting in a significantly inferior user experience. For the past decade, blockchain has struggled to compete with mobile payments in human-dominated scenarios such as daily small-amount transfers and face-to-face payments. However, in high-frequency, automated, contract-driven payment scenarios involving AI agents, blockchain's advantages are far superior. Intelligent agents do not require QR codes or manual confirmation. They require payments to be executed automatically. Once preset conditions are met, the smart contract immediately triggers the transfer, without any intermediary intervention. Programmability allows payments to embed complex logic: funds are only released when agent A delivers the specified content, agent B completes data verification, and an external oracle confirms that the market price has reached a threshold. If any step fails, the transaction automatically rolls back. Blockchain supports 24/7 operation, instant settlement between any addresses globally, and instant finality for every transaction. These capabilities are currently completely unavailable from traditional banking systems and mobile payment platforms. Multi-agent organizations will become the "main arena" for blockchain payments. Mobile payments initially had no significant advantage in face-to-face payments. At that time, people were still accustomed to cash and card payments, and mobile payments were even considered redundant in small shops. However, it found a breakthrough in e-commerce scenarios. Order payments on Taobao and JD.com require instant online settlement and support for massive concurrency, where mobile payments quickly gained a foothold. It first refined the e-commerce payment experience to its extreme, accumulating users, merchants, and network effects before giving back to the whole society. The ease with which we make payments today stems from mobile payment's initial success in e-commerce. AI multi-agent organizations will become the most solid and explosive foundation for blockchain payments and value exchange. Here, payment is no longer an occasional human act, but a regular part of system operation. It could be micro-payments occurring every second, computing power rental fees, model call fees, data usage fees, or intellectual property sharing. These payments require embedded complex conditions and atomic-level execution. Traditional payment infrastructure struggles to cope, while blockchain is naturally suited. It doesn't need to change user habits because the intelligent agent itself is code. It doesn't need customer service support because everything is guaranteed by contracts. It doesn't need centralized risk control because trust is provided by cryptography and distributed consensus. I believe this precisely reflects the penetrating nature of blockchain technology. It establishes irreplaceable structural advantages in the scenarios where it is most needed. Blockchain does not need to completely replace existing payment systems. It only needs to take root in places where humans don't yet need it, building a completely new value network. As AI agents work in large groups, this network will grow rapidly, gradually extending from micro-payments between agents to broader economic activities, ultimately benefiting human society. Mobile payment proved itself with e-commerce; blockchain will prove itself with AI multi-agent organizations. When the base of agent payments is firmly established, the position of blockchain in the entire digital economy will be completely different. The New Form of the AI ​​Economy The combination of AI multi-agent organizations and blockchain is far more than just a technological overlap. It will open up a completely new economic landscape, profoundly changing resource allocation, social exchange, individual income, and the innovation ecosystem. The following analysis will explore this from four dimensions. First, it will significantly improve the overall performance and resource allocation efficiency of AI multi-agent systems. Currently, most multi-agent applications are still in the "playing house" stage. Developers mainly rely on agent skills, hooks, MCP (Multi-Channel Programming), prompt engineering, and other methods to simulate and customize personalized "digital employees." This is essentially a primitive role-playing state; everyone is using prompts to create seemingly professional AI characters, then having them chat and divide tasks to simulate a multi-step workflow. It looks lively, but the actual advantage compared to using a single, all-powerful AI assistant is quite limited. True multi-agent organizations are completely different. Some agents will possess unique resources and capabilities that cannot be easily imitated or replaced through simple customization. These capabilities may include proprietary datasets, exclusive model weights, real-time data sources specific to a particular domain, high-precision simulation environments, or industry experience accumulated through long-term training. They can only be developed, cultivated, and released by institutions with unique resources. Calling these advanced agents inevitably involves real payments. The key role of smart contracts in blockchain lies in this. They can encapsulate call rules, pricing mechanisms, quality verification, and fee settlement all within the code. Once conditions are met, payment is automatically triggered, and resources are automatically delivered; if conditions are not met, funds are automatically rolled back. The entire process is efficient, secure, programmable, and auditable. The inefficient methods of manual negotiation, email confirmation, and post-event reconciliation will completely disappear. Resource allocation efficiency will thus be significantly improved, and the overall performance of the multi-agent system will reach a new level. This is not simply a cost reduction, but a true expansion of the system's capabilities. Secondly, it significantly promotes the scale of social exchange and the speed of economic growth. Traditional financial infrastructure sets extremely high barriers to transactions with small, frequent, and complex conditions. Bank transfers have minimum amount limits, clearing has time windows, and cross-border payments involve exchange rates and compliance costs. These frictions directly exclude a large number of potential transactions. When blockchain provides AI agents with a low-friction micro-payment and value exchange network, the situation will fundamentally change. Agents can easily complete instant settlements for a few cents at a time for computing power leasing, data calls, model fine-tuning services, and even single API calls. Transactions that were previously suppressed due to high costs are now feasible. Massive amounts of previously impossible exchanges will be released, significantly accelerating the speed and scale of economic cycles. Imagine: a content creation agent automatically pays a small copyright fee to the material-providing agent for every piece of high-quality text it generates; an investment analysis agent pays a fee to the data source agent for every real-time market data it accesses; a logistics optimization agent pays a corresponding reward to the map service agent for every route planning it completes. These micro-payments accumulate to form an extremely large value flow network. The density and frequency of economic activity will increase, and overall economic growth will gain new momentum. Third, enabling ordinary people to truly earn money through AI agents will address the structural gap in supply and demand matching in the AI ​​era. One of the most prominent contradictions in the AI ​​era is that large model companies possess core capabilities, while the supply and demand of a large number of ordinary people are difficult to effectively match. Many people have unique data, experience, or scenarios, but lack the ability to transform them into AI services; at the same time, there are many tasks that require specialized agents to complete, but suitable service providers cannot be found. The "one person, multiple agents" model will become a new form of employment. Ordinary people can deploy and operate their own agent networks, encapsulating their personal knowledge, data, or industry insights into callable agent modules, and then providing services and automatically collecting payments through a blockchain network. Some people are skilled in local life services and can train regional life assistant agents; others are familiar with a niche field and can develop specialized analytical agents for that field. These agents are no longer free toys, but economic units capable of autonomously generating income. In this way, a new balance mechanism will be formed in the supply and demand matching of the AI ​​era. The supply side will no longer be dominated by a few large companies, and the demand side can also accurately reach the most suitable intelligent agent services through micro-payments. Ordinary people will no longer just be consumers of AI, but can become contributors and beneficiaries of the AI ​​value network. This will greatly alleviate the employment pressure brought about by AI, while allowing the innovation vitality of the whole society to be more fully released. Fourth, prevent large AI model companies from evolving into new economic oligarchs. Currently, there is a serious imbalance in the AI ​​ecosystem. All technologies such as prompt engineering and skills that embody knowledge and experience exist almost entirely in the form of free and open source, making it difficult to obtain sustained economic incentives. Developers are desperately burning tokens, only to receive cheap cheers on social networks, which are difficult to convert into income. Only large model companies have clear business models, and they control the underlying computing power and basic models. Even more dangerous is that once large model companies observe a successful pattern, they can easily replicate or even surpass existing AI startups with just a slight adjustment at the model layer. This leads to the rapid elimination of numerous innovative teams, and the innovation ecosystem faces the risk of being exploited. When Anthropic released its Managed Agents product in early April, some lamented that at least 1,000 startups woke up to find their value zero. The situation will change when the Agent itself becomes an economic element capable of autonomously receiving payments. The value network will be decentralized and restructured. Each intelligent agent can independently price, settle, and accumulate reputation and assets through the blockchain. Successful Agents will no longer be dependent on a large model but will become independent nodes in the network. Developers can earn direct revenue by continuously iterating their Agents without having to relinquish all value to the underlying model provider. Large model companies will remain important, but they will transform from rule-makers into infrastructure providers. Their overwhelming advantages will be effectively balanced, and the diversity and vitality of the innovation ecosystem will be protected. This is not a denial of large-scale companies, but rather a move to make the entire AI economic system healthier and more sustainable. The combination of AI and blockchain is painting an unprecedented economic picture for us. In this picture, resource allocation is more precise, exchanges are smoother, ordinary people have new sources of income, and the innovation ecosystem remains open and vibrant. The benefits brought by this integration far exceed the technology itself. It will profoundly affect our production methods and wealth distribution patterns over the next twenty years. Short-term obstacles and realistic prudence: While the trend is clear, the implementation process will not be smooth sailing. The combination of AI multi-agent organizations and blockchain faces several real and thorny obstacles. We must face them squarely to avoid blind optimism. First, although the US has taken the lead in digital asset legislation, it has not yet been fully implemented. The CLARITY Act is stalled, with the Treasury and regulatory agencies continuing to push forward with details on anti-money laundering and reserve asset management. Traditional powers like banks still face resistance to stablecoin issuance and smart contract payments. The refinement of the regulatory framework takes time, and uncertainties in its implementation will continue to constrain large-scale application in the short term. Secondly, other countries, including China, maintain a conflicted regulatory stance. Many economies are concerned about monetary sovereignty, capital flows, and financial stability, making it difficult to quickly introduce clear and favorable frameworks in the short term. Legislative delays and policy instability will create additional friction for cross-jurisdictional collaboration among intelligent agents. Thirdly, AI practitioners generally lack understanding of blockchain and even hold cognitive biases. In the AI ​​community, blockchain is often simply equated with speculation or even fraud. Many developers only see on-chain gas fees and confirmation delays, rarely understanding the structural value of distributed ledgers and smart contracts for multi-agent organizations. This cognitive gap leads to a reluctance of top AI talent to invest in the field, slowing down the progress of integration projects. Meanwhile, the blockchain industry is currently experiencing a downturn in funding, talent, and confidence. The shadows cast by fraud and project failures following the bursting of the speculative bubble in recent years have not yet fully dissipated. High-quality projects are struggling to secure funding, top engineers are leaving in large numbers, and the overall industry confidence will take time to recover. If these problems are not handled properly, they could amplify external biases and further slow down the integration with AI. These obstacles are real and unlikely to be completely eliminated in the short term. They remind us that any major technological integration is not a linear process but requires repeated breakthroughs in cognition, regulation, and practice. However, it is precisely because these obstacles exist that the strategic significance of this integration becomes even more prominent. Whoever can break through cognitive bottlenecks first, proactively fill regulatory gaps, and invest resources in cultivating cross-disciplinary talent will gain a competitive advantage in the new paradigm of AI and blockchain integration. Returning to the essence of technology, setting aside short-term noise, and investing in genuine cognitive upgrades and practical exploration is the only way to address this challenge. History has repeatedly proven that at major technological turning points in a great era, those who hesitate and observe often miss the window of opportunity, while those who dare to face obstacles and continuously iterate ultimately become the driving force behind the wave. The trend is set. The development of AI multi-agent organizations will inevitably lead to the deep integration of AI and blockchain. This is not a possibility, but a historical inevitability driven by technological logic. Intelligent agents need automatic payments triggered by high-frequency, minute, and complex conditions, and blockchain provides the most suitable infrastructure. Due to its relatively advanced digital asset legislation, the United States has already accumulated a significant first-mover advantage in stablecoins and on-chain infrastructure. In the coming years, they are likely to transform this integration into real productivity and economic control. China must not be complacent in this integration. Considering the talent pool, technological reserves, and resource investment of China and the United States in core AI technologies, the gap between the two sides is not significant in the competition of models and applications alone. If, several years from now, China's AI ecosystem lags significantly behind that of the United States, it will undoubtedly be because the US has done things that China cannot or is unable to do. Currently, blockchain payments seem to be such a case.

data.title: The biggest highlight of Musk's version of WeChat has nothing to do with WeChat itself.
data.description:
Only three days remain until Musk's "super app dream" comes true. On April 17th, XChat is expected to officially launch on the Apple App Store, with simultaneous global downloads.

This is definitely not an ordinary app launch. On Musk's crazy and massive business strategy chessboard, XChat is another core and most indispensable move after his $44 billion acquisition of Twitter and its violent renaming to X.

His anticipation for this day dates back to 2022. After acquiring Twitter, Musk mentioned WeChat in almost every public appearance. He said, "In China, you basically live in WeChat because it's so useful for daily life. If we can achieve that on X, even just get close, it will be a huge success." Musk isn't obsessed with WeChat's chat interface, but with its status as a digital life operating system—payments, communication, ride-hailing, food delivery, utility bills, all in one app. If acquiring Twitter was his ticket to the super app gamble, then XChat is the first card he's played. Despite being touted as Musk's version of WeChat, XChat has become more like Telegram? Functionally, XChat focuses on privacy-first, standalone chat application. Registration does not require a phone number; simply log in with your X account. Messages support self-destructing messages, recall, and editing. Group chats can accommodate up to 481 people, and file transfers have a maximum limit of 4GB. Cross-device audio and video calls are all built-in. Download requires iOS 26.0 or later. The application-level prohibition of screenshots and screen recording attempts to plug the loopholes for content leakage at the source. This may be a favorite feature of some tech industry bosses. Grok AI is directly embedded in the chat interface and can be called up at any time in the conversation for summarizing content, real-time translation, or planning itineraries. XChat's overall positioning follows a clean, private, and minimally disruptive approach. The interface is stripped of the main X app's feed, ads, and trending topics, leaving dedicated space for private conversations. It initially supports 46 languages, including Simplified and Traditional Chinese. Bearing Musk's distinct personal imprint, XChat not only makes bold changes to its positioning but also progresses at an astonishing speed, even revealing a degree of radicalism and fervor. Last June, Musk publicly teased X; by December, X employee Nikita Bier was already publicly endorsing it, marveling at how the team "completed the encrypted private messaging migration in just three months," and taking a jab at competitors: "Facebook took three years to do this." This March, the iOS version of TestFlight opened for testing, initially with 1000 participants, quickly expanding to... The 5,000 slots were snapped up within two hours of the announcement, but the high level of attention was accompanied by extremely polarized reviews. @Nicole_yang88, a user who received her TestFlight qualification in March, wrote: "The overall smoothness is extremely high, with almost no lag. The interface follows a minimalist approach, with clear hierarchy and restrained color scheme, and it does indeed resemble the clean style of iMessage." She also specifically mentioned the seamless integration of one-click authorization login with the main X app and account data, "without any sense of disconnect when switching apps."

But some people don't buy it at all. Test user @ohxiyu posted: "Upon opening it, it's pixel-perfectly identical to X's private messages. So why separate it? Private messages, requests, and harassment are all mixed together, no different from current DMs. Want to chat with someone? There's no contact list; you can only search through chat history." Even more baffling is the design of the private mode. If the other party has enabled self-destructing messages, you receive no notification, and the content disappears after a short while. He said, "At least Telegram pops up a notification. There's not even a menu. It feels like they just put a DM page in a shell and threw it out." Even before XChat was officially available for download, trouble had already begun. On April 11th, the day pre-orders opened, users issued warnings: a Russian version of XChat had appeared on the App Store, with an icon and name highly similar to the real product. After downloading, it would require users to provide credit card information and ID to prove their age. The image on the right is the official version and a safe download. Currently, the only reliable path is through the official Apple App Store (overseas version), ensuring the developer is X Corp. Blogger @Imlaomao personally experienced this issue: "After accidentally entering my credit card information, I realized something was wrong and immediately canceled all my credit cards." While he stated there was no direct evidence to prove the app definitely had a problem, he advised everyone to "prioritize safety and be cautious." Is the so-called "Bitcoin-style encryption" just a word game? In all of XChat's promotional rhetoric, "Bitcoin-style encryption" is undoubtedly the most eye-catching phrase. Musk, a master of traffic generation, successfully used this somewhat geeky term to conjure up a cyberpunk-like image in countless people's minds: chat logs on the blockchain, decentralized storage. The ideal is often far from reality. According to Nvidia security developer Matthew Garrett's technical analysis of early versions of XChat, XChat's message encryption layer uses libsodium's box encryption scheme. This scheme itself has undergone extensive auditing and can be considered robust. However, Musk didn't clarify one point: libsodium's core is written in C, and X uses the C version, not the "completely new Rust architecture" he claimed. Regarding key management, XChat uses the open-source protocol Juicebox—this protocol has its own white paper and wasn't developed by X itself. Its design involves encrypting your private key, splitting it into fragments, and storing them on multiple servers controlled by X. When you switch to a new device, you enter a 4-digit PIN code, the system retrieves the fragments from the servers, reassembles the key, and restores all chat history. The problem is that X's three backend domains are all under x.com, presumably directly controlled by Twitter. The Juicebox protocol itself supports introducing independent third-party backends to distribute trust, but Garrett's analysis found no substantial deployment of this feature in X. Even more critically, XChat's protocol lacks "forward secrecy." This means that if your static key is compromised one day—whether your device is stolen, your key is seized, or the server reassembles and decrypts it—all your past chat history will be instantly accessible. Signal's "Double Ratchet" algorithm ensures that even if a communication key is leaked once, your history remains secure. XChat does not have this mechanism. Furthermore, by checking the privacy labels disclosed by Apple's App Store, users discovered that XChat retains the right to collect and associate data with user identities, covering contact information, communication content, usage data, diagnostic data, and user IDs. In contrast, Signal only collects a very small amount of contact information necessary for registration and never associates it with individual identities. A deeper issue lies in metadata. XChat may encrypt the text and images you send, but the X platform records in its backend: who you chat with, the frequency of your chats, the most active time periods, and the size of the files transferred. In the contemporary data economy, the commercial value of metadata often exceeds that of the content itself. These behavioral trajectories can feed back into X's main site's advertising engine and are also excellent corpus for training Grok AI. In short, encrypted chat content and exposed behavioral data constitute XChat's biggest privacy paradox. The real intention is not what it seems; Musk's ambition for a super app is more than just an ad. To understand XChat's ambition, one must first understand what Musk truly wants to achieve. His elaborate efforts suggest he doesn't just want a simple chat tool, but a "super app" that integrates users' daily lives—a place to talk to friends, transfer money, and buy things. Following this logic, XChat is just the first step. It aims for deep integration with the upcoming X Money payment system, allowing users to complete cross-border remittances and daily transfers while sending messages, completely creating a closed-loop business model of "social + payment." However, the obstacle lies in regulation. The United States lacks a unified federal financial remittance license; applications must be submitted individually in each of the fifty states. As of early 2026, X Payments LLC had obtained licenses in over 40 states and Washington, D.C., but New York State, the heart of North American finance, remains closed to Musk.

New York State Senator Brad Hoylman-Sigal and Representative Micah Lasher jointly submitted an open letter to the New York Department of Financial Services, using strong language, demanding that a license be denied to X, arguing that Musk's "behavior is seriously lacking in character and general suitability." For a payment network aiming for the entire US and even the world, losing New York State means XChat's payment network can no longer cover the most affluent consumers in the country. Moreover, Western users are inherently averse to "putting all their eggs in one basket," and if payment functionality is further compromised, the story becomes even more untenable. While these setbacks certainly raise doubts about the "super app" narrative, once the underlying logic is understood, everything becomes reasonable.

Leaving aside the geeky hype about self-destructing messages and encrypted privacy, the most imaginative rumor about XChat right now is how it will integrate with its own AI big model, Grok.

While we can't actually verify this yet, if we follow this line of thought, you'll find that what Musk truly wants to disrupt isn't the chat experience, but the underlying logic of human-computer interaction—that is, what should a super app look like in the AI ​​era? WeChat's super app model can be summarized as "entry point aggregation": one app crams in travel, food delivery, payment, social security, and health, allowing users to navigate between different services within a single interface. This model has essentially defined the product paradigm of the Chinese internet over the past decade. But its underlying logic remains "you come to find services." You know you need a ride, so you click on the Didi mini-program; you know you need to pay, so you open WeChat Pay. However, entry point aggregation is the answer for super apps in the app era. AI scheduling may be the true answer to the super app of the AI ​​era. Instead of cramming a hundred functions into users, let one AI handle everything for them. Of course, based on current leaks, XChat is still far from this vision. Without a rich service ecosystem to support it, even the smartest Grok can only do menial tasks like translation and text summarization within the chat box. Musk's answer may be rough and controversial, but he has begun to submit his paper.

data.title: Assassination list exposed: 20-year-old suspect becomes killer "to save all mankind"
data.description:
There's a scene in the sci-fi anime *Pantheon* where the anti-AI organization "AIDAS" obtains a list of all the scientists and executives promoting consciousness-uploading technology, and then takes action against them one by one.

When watching, viewers felt this was an extreme setting in the animation.

4 AM, April 11, 2026, San Francisco.

San Francisco.

A 20-year-old Texas boy named Daniel Moreno-Gama, wearing a Champion hoodie and carrying a kerosene canister, stood in front of Ultraman's multi-million dollar mansion, lit a Molotov cocktail, and threw it out.

An hour and a half later, he appeared at OpenAI headquarters, picked up a chair, smashed the glass door, and shouted at the security guards, "I'm going to burn this place down and kill everyone inside."

At that moment, the Pantheon pierced into reality.

The animated film became a real news headline. The assassination list went from fiction to reality.

The animated film became a real news headline. The assassination list went from fiction to reality.

Suspect Profile: From PauseAI Forum to Molotov Cocktails

Suspect Profile: From PauseAI Forum to Molotov Cocktails

This boy is named Daniel Moreno-Gama, 20 years old, from Spring, Texas.

Suspect Daniel Moreno-Gama, wearing a black Champion hoodie and holding a transparent container resembling a kerosene can, stands at the building entrance. This blurry screenshot almost completely overlaps with the silhouette of the AIDAS killer in Pantheon, sending chills down one's spine.

He flew from Texas to San Francisco specifically for this one night.

The FBI found the complete tools used in the crime on him: a fire extinguisher, a can of kerosene, a blue lighter, and a chilling "anti-AI manifesto." The manifesto's title was clearly "Your Final Warning," followed by his signature. It explicitly stated the intention to "kill/attempt to kill" Victim 1, namely OpenAI CEO Altman. Even more terrifying is this statement: "If I were to encourage others to kill and commit crimes, then I must lead by example and demonstrate that my message is entirely sincere." This is not the ramblings of a madman. This is a declaration of war. On Monday, FBI agents went to Daniel Moreno-Gama's home in Spring, a suburb of Houston, Texas, and stayed there for several hours before leaving. The investigation revealed that two years ago, he joined PauseAI's Discord forum and posted 34 messages. While he didn't directly make any threats, one of them was flagged as a "vague threat." PauseAI's goal is to persuade governments to intervene and suspend the development of Superhuman AI. The non-profit organization that called for a halt to cutting-edge AI development is now a prime target for FBI investigation. From keyboard rage to real-life flames, he finally took that step. That list: Silicon Valley's "Schindler's List" turned upside down. What truly kept Silicon Valley up at night was another part of the list. It was a detailed list. It listed the names and home addresses of numerous AI company board members, CEOs, and investors. This is no longer simply a matter of personal venting. It's a cold-blooded "target selection logic"—first target the most representative individuals, then incite more people to follow suit. Federal prosecutors have issued a strong statement: if it is proven that his intention was to coerce the government or change public policy, this case will be directly classified as domestic terrorism. The AIDAS organization from *The Pantheon* is now a reality in Silicon Valley. The distance between fiction and reality has been reduced to zero. This is not an isolated incident. This is a clear turning point where AI panic has escalated from online petitions and street protests to offline violence. Movements like PauseAI originally called for a halt and for safety. Today, some people interpret "safety" as something that must be achieved using kerosene and flames. Public warnings from Anthropic executives and others—that AI could lead to 30%-50% of human unemployment—are objectively providing extremists with a "moral license." A dangerous logical loop has formed: AI threatens human survival → stopping AI is justice → stopping AI by force is also necessary justice. This landslide has already reached the bottom for some people. When the speed of technological advancement exceeds the psychological capacity of society, fear begins to backfire.

AI overhype has backfired

Do you think AI is just a tool?

Now, it has become a "human extinction machine" in the eyes of some people.

Their chosen method of response was true "extermination"—first eliminating those who instigated it. Who is the next target? Ultraman has confirmed the attack. He even posted a photo of his husband and young child, writing, "Hopefully this will deter the next person who wants to throw a Molotov cocktail at my house...whatever you think of me." He acknowledged that "fear and anxiety about AI are reasonable," but emphasized the need to "reduce rhetoric and tactics to prevent further explosions in more homes." Security levels in Silicon Valley are being urgently upgraded. But those names on the list are probably having a hard time sleeping right now. The deeper question is: When those who fear AI begin to respond with violence, and those who are pushing AI forward continue to accelerate recklessly—who will apply the brakes? This fear and confrontation triggered by technology has only just entered its most dangerous phase. And who will be the next target?

data.title: A Triumph of Values: How Anthropic Overtakes OpenAI
data.description: This might be the most thrilling AI revenge drama of the year. OpenAI, once the undisputed king of big data analytics, is no longer the dominant force it once was. Its former employees, along with six others, have left to found Anthropic, which is eroding OpenAI's leading position in terms of revenue, valuation, and market share. The contrast in the secondary market is most striking. Ken Smythe, founder of Next Round Capital, has $600 million in applications to sell existing OpenAI shares, with six hedge funds and venture capital firms lining up to sell. At this time last year, these shares would have been snapped up within days. Now? He's sifted through a pool of hundreds of institutional investors, but can't find a single buyer. At the same time, $2 billion in cash is queuing up to buy Anthropic shares. On the on-chain derivatives platform Ventuals, Anthropic's implied valuation briefly surpassed OpenAI's, at $863.6 billion to $846.1 billion. Goldman Sachs' attitude is even more telling. Selling existing OpenAI shares to high-net-worth clients without taking a share of the profits is essentially a discounted fire sale. But selling Anthropic shares? They still take a 15% to 20% carry, take it or leave it. How did Anthropic, founded only five years ago, gradually surpass its former parent company, OpenAI? The story begins in 2020. That year, Dario Amodei was the VP of Research at OpenAI, involved in building GPT-2 and GPT-3. Many versions circulated in Silicon Valley regarding his departure; some said it was because Microsoft's investment changed the nature of OpenAI, others said it was due to fundamental differences in security philosophy. Dario himself discussed this issue on Lex Fridman's podcast, essentially saying: arguing about other people's visions is extremely inefficient; instead of trying to change others, take the people you trust and do what you want to do. In 2021, Dario left with his sister Daniela and five other core OpenAI researchers to found Anthropic. Sam Altman probably didn't pay much attention at the time. OpenAI was at its peak then; losing a few researchers wasn't a big deal. However, at the height of the "board coup" in November 2023, OpenAI's board even approached Dario, asking if he would be willing to replace Altman as CEO and, incidentally, merge the two companies. Dario refused. He didn't want the OpenAI CEO position; he wanted to build something from scratch according to his own logic. From 2021 to 2024, Anthropic was virtually invisible to the outside world. When ChatGPT exploded globally at the end of 2022, Claude was still in internal testing. The Anthropic team believed that safety was more important than functionality. This insistence caused them to miss the first wave of AI hype, but it also gave them a unique position in the subsequent competition. A Comeback! The numbers speak for themselves. Anthropic's Annualized Revenue (ARR): $1 billion in January 2025, $9 billion by the end of the year, $14 billion in February 2026, $19 billion in March, and surpassing $30 billion in early April. OpenAI's comparison: approximately $13 billion in 2025, and approximately $25 billion by April 2026. Anthropic grew 30 times in 15 months, going from lagging behind OpenAI by an order of magnitude to surpassing it by 20%. OpenAI's own growth is also quite rapid, but compared to Anthropic, it becomes a contrast of "steady growth vs. exponential growth." The biggest structural difference lies in the fact that over 80% of OpenAI's revenue comes from ChatGPT consumer subscriptions. 900 million weekly active users—the numbers are impressive—but the paid subscription rate is only around 5%, with the remaining 95% simply using computing power for free. Anthropic, on the other hand, derives 80% of its revenue from enterprise clients and API calls. Enterprise revenue and consumer revenue are completely different things. Enterprise contracts are difficult to switch to once signed; deep integration incurs switching costs, resulting in high renewal rates and year-on-year increases in subscription amounts. Consumer subscriptions can be canceled at any time; a new feature can cause a significant loss of subscribers. In trading terms, one is a long-duration asset, and the other is a short-duration asset. Let's look at some specific data. By April 2026, Anthropic had over 1,000 enterprise clients with annual fees exceeding $1 million, doubling in two months. Eight of the Fortune 10 companies use Claude. In the core field of code generation, Claude captured 42% to 54% of the global market share, while OpenAI only had 21%. Ramp's enterprise spending data shows that Anthropic's share of enterprise AI spending surged from 10% at the beginning of 2025 to over 65% in February 2026. Do these figures mean OpenAI is "failing"? Not necessarily. But they do illustrate one thing: the first-mover advantage that everyone thought was unshakeable a year ago—brand, user base, and ecosystem—has played almost no role in the enterprise market; enterprise purchasing decisions follow a different logic. The catalyst for Anthropic's explosive revenue growth was a product called Claude Code. Released in May 2025, it surpassed $1 billion in annualized revenue by November, and exceeded $2.5 billion by February 2026. A product went from zero to $2.5 billion in just nine months. Looking back at the history of the SaaS industry, there's no faster example. Cursor took over a year to reach $500 million, and GitHub Copilot took even longer. What exactly is the difference between Claude Code and previous AI programming tools? Simply put, GitHub Copilot completes the next line of code for you while you write it, so you're still the one doing the work. Claude Code, on the other hand, lets you tell it "I want a user login module," and it writes the code, creates the files, runs tests, and commits changes all by itself, while you watch from the sidelines. This difference may seem like a matter of degree, but it's actually a paradigm shift: one is a "better tool," the other is a "colleague who replaces you in doing the work." Anthropic's internal data further illustrates this point. Boris Cherny, the head of Claude Code, says that 100% of his daily code is now written using Claude Code, and 70% to 90% of the entire engineering team's code is generated by it. 90% of Claude Code's own codebase is also written by themselves. A Pragmatic Engineer survey of 15,000 developers in February 2026 ranked Claude Code as the most popular AI coding tool. By early 2026, 4% of public commits on GitHub were made using Claude Code, and this figure was projected to exceed 20% by the end of the year. Claude Code's success reveals a reality many in the AI ​​industry are unwilling to face: the commercial ceiling for chatbots themselves may be quite low. What truly motivates companies to invest heavily are AI tools that are embedded in workflows and replace specific job functions. ChatGPT opened the door to AI, but whether one chooses to go left or right after entering determines who can turn users into revenue. Anthropic went right, entering the production process of enterprises. In January 2026, Anthropic released Cowork, extending the same approach from developers to all white-collar jobs. Four engineers built it in 10 days, with most of the code written by Claude Code himself. Since the launch of Claude Cowork, the global SaaS sector has lost approximately $2 trillion in market value. The differences between product and strategy are obvious, but the real key lies in: people. Looking at OpenAI, from 2024 to 2025, the company experienced a systemic exodus of senior executives. Co-founder and Chief Scientist Ilya Sutskever left to found Safe Superintelligence. CTO Mira Murati left to found Thinking Machines Lab. Co-founder John Schulman and Super Alignment Team leader Jan Leike went to Anthropic. Chief Research Officer Bob McGrew left, VP of Research Barret Zoph left, and co-founder and president Greg Brockman took an extended leave of absence. In the summer of 2025, at least seven researchers were poached by Meta's Super Intelligence Lab. Of OpenAI's original 11 co-founders, only Sam Altman and researcher Wojciech Zaremba remained full-time by the end of 2025. A former employee told Fortune: "OpenAI without Ilya is a different company; OpenAI without Greg is a very different company." Anthropic presents a different picture. All seven co-founders—Dario Amodei, Daniela Amodei, Jared Kaplan, Jack Clark, Sam McCandlish, Ben Mann, and Tom Brown—are still active, and there hasn't been a single publicly announced departure at the executive level in the five years since the company's founding. This contrast is stark, prompting the question: What exactly did Anthropic do to keep them on the team? Forbes estimated in early 2026 that each of the seven co-founders would hold approximately 1.8% of the shares, a very small difference. At a valuation of $380 billion, each person's shares would be worth approximately $6.8 billion. This near-equal shareholding structure is completely different from the common practice in Silicon Valley, where the CEO typically holds the majority, with the remaining founders receiving progressively less. Equal shareholding eliminates at least one of the most common sources of conflict within the founding team: who feels they are being shortchanged. Equity is just the surface; what's more noteworthy is Dario Amodei's time commitment to management. He mentioned on the Dwarkesh Podcast that he spends about one-third to 40% of his time "ensuring the culture at Anthropic is good." This is unusually high for an AI company CEO. As the company expanded to 2,500 people, he could no longer participate in every technical and product decision, so he chose to focus his energy on something more "leveraged": aligning everyone's direction. How exactly does he do this? He holds a company-wide meeting every two weeks, internally called "DVQ"—Dario Vision Quest. This name was given by employees; Dario himself once considered changing it because it sounded like a hallucinogenic experience. For each meeting, he prepares three or four pages of documentation and speaks for an hour in front of the entire company, covering topics ranging from product strategy and geopolitics to his assessment of the AI ​​industry's future. Most employees attend in person or remotely. On a more routine level, Anthropic has a Slack culture akin to "notebook channels." Every employee, including Dario himself, maintains a public Slack channel to share their thoughts, work progress, and even questions. In an interview with Fortune, he said something to the effect of: "My goal is to build a reputation for 'telling the company the truth,' directly pointing out problems and avoiding 'corporate speak' (that defensive, politically correct corporate language). If you hire people you trust, you can communicate completely unfiltered." This "anti-PR" style of internal communication contrasts sharply with OpenAI. During OpenAI's board crisis at the end of 2023, internal information was so fragmented that even the CTO was unsure what was happening. Anthropic's cultural filtering begins from the recruitment stage. Every candidate, regardless of the position they apply for, undergoes a standardized "culture interview." Only employees who have been with the company for at least 30 days and completed multi-stage culture training are qualified to serve as culture interviewers. The logic is: cultural transmission is too important to be entrusted to someone who doesn't even understand what the company culture is. Reportedly, one question in the culture interview was: If Anthropic decides not to release a model due to its inability to guarantee security, and your equity is reduced to zero, would you be willing to accept it? This isn't a rhetorical question; no matter how strong your technical skills, if you can't answer this question, you won't be hired. Another detail: All technical positions at Anthropic, from new hires to founding executives, use the same title: "Member of Technical Staff." There are no hierarchical distinctions like "Senior," "Chief," or "Distinguished." Employees internally refer to each other as "ants" (an abbreviation of Anthropic). The company even hired a full-time philosopher, Amanda Askell, whose job was to shape Claude's framework of moral judgment. She once told Time: "Sometimes it feels like you have a 6-year-old child, and you're teaching it what kindness is, but when it's 15, it's smarter than you in every way." Daniela Amodei's role in this system is often underestimated. Dario is the technical visionary and external advocate, while Daniela oversees execution, culture, talent, and operational infrastructure. According to reports, her executive teams in research, product, sales, and operations all report directly to her. She has a clear preference in hiring: people with strong communication skills, high emotional intelligence, kindness, curiosity, and a willingness to help others. This emphasis on "soft skills" is uncommon in an industry dominated by tech founders. All seven founders of Anthropic have signed a pledge to donate 80% of their wealth, and nearly 30 Anthropic employees have registered to attend the 2026 EA (Effective Altruism) conference in San Francisco—more than double the combined attendance of OpenAI, Google DeepMind, xAI, and Meta Superintelligence Labs. The core asset of an AI company is the human brain. Code can be copied, computing power can be bought, but the intuition and judgment of researchers cannot be taken away. When your chief scientist, CTO, and chief research officer leave within two years, what you lose cannot be measured in terms of funding. Anthropic's stability in terms of talent is perhaps its most difficult advantage to replicate. All victories are victories of values. What happened to OpenAI? At this point, I need to say a few words in defense of OpenAI. Anthropic's revenue has surpassed OpenAI's, and secondary market sentiment is shifting. But OpenAI hasn't collapsed. It just completed a $122 billion funding round, with participants including Amazon, Nvidia, SoftBank, and Microsoft. ChatGPT still has 900 million weekly active users. In consumers' minds, "AI" and "ChatGPT" are almost synonymous, but OpenAI does have some structural problems, and these problems will all surface in 2026. The financial pressure is the most direct. OpenAI projects a loss of $14 billion in 2026. Cumulative losses from 2023 to 2028 could reach as high as $44 billion. HSBC analysts believe profitability will not arrive before 2030. The Wall Street Journal estimates that OpenAI's annual training costs will reach $125 billion by 2030, compared to approximately $30 billion for Anthropic during the same period. Both are training cutting-edge models, yet the costs differ by four times; this gap needs explanation. Part of the reason is OpenAI's more aggressive investment in computing infrastructure, and part of it may be efficiency issues. The capital market is clearly very concerned about this gap. Anthropic expects to achieve positive cash flow in 2027, while OpenAI has pushed its break-even point to 2030. There have also been some issues at the product level. Sora shut down in March 2026. This video generation tool reportedly had operating costs of $15 million per day and total revenue of $2.1 million. The shutdown also ruined a partnership with Disney, reportedly canceling a $1 billion investment deal. OpenAI's new AGI deployment head, Fidji Simo, bluntly told employees that the company "cannot afford to be distracted by side projects." Then there's advertising. In February 2026, OpenAI added ads to the free and Go versions of ChatGPT. This event itself isn't big news; many products have advertising models. However, it's particularly jarring in the context of OpenAI because Sam Altman explicitly stated in 2024 that advertising was a "last resort," and that the combination of advertising and AI made him "uniquely uneasy." From this "unique unease" to the "official launch," only 15 months passed. With only 5% of 900 million users paying, this figure forced him to make this choice. The corporate governance aspect is even more complex. The restructuring from non-profit to for-profit dragged on for nearly a year. There was Elon Musk's lawsuit, a joint letter of opposition from former employees, an open letter signed by a Nobel laureate, and reviews from the attorneys general of California and Delaware. The restructuring was finally completed in October 2025, with the non-profit foundation retaining a 26% stake and control. Critics argue that this arrangement is merely a formality. Individually, none of these events are fatal. Taken together, they paint a less-than-ideal picture: a company that once led the industry's imagination is now filled with internal governance struggles, product shutdowns, and advertising. Anthropic's momentum is indeed formidable. Revenue surpassing others, secondary market frenzy, and free global PR from the Pentagon incident. But one thing is worth remembering: if you asked any industry analyst at the end of 2023, "Will OpenAI be surpassed?", 99% would say no. The rapid shift in consensus should itself raise concerns about the current new consensus. A few things are relatively certain. Anthropic's enterprise-centric approach is correct; its 80% enterprise revenue structure is far healthier than ChatGPT's consumer model, supported by robust financial data.
